was...<|12.24|> <|12.24|> Well, thanks.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> It was August 13th, 2017.<|19.60|> <|19.60|> And as our lectionary readings come about every three years, I preached on this same<|24.60|> <|24.60|> text from Matthew's Gospel.<|27.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I'd been at St. John's for just a little under a month, and I was learning so much about<|8.76|> <|8.76|> this place and about this community.<|12.38|> <|12.38|> You know that expression, trying to drink from a fire hose?<|16.62|> <|16.62|> That was indeed how it felt.<|18.70|> of the time when we hear this story read or spoken about, the focus is on Peter.<|23.36|> <|23.36|> Peter.<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> His action is often portrayed as some kind of self-help manifesto or preached as a morality tale.<|10.46|> <|12.06|> And I don't like either of those.<|14.72|> <|15.98|> So what struck me for the first time when I read this, starting here at St. John's six years ago,<|24.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> was to pay attention to the disciples,<|5.00|> <|5.34|> to those who stayed in the boat.<|8.98|> <|9.84|> Though the winds were fierce, though the waves were large,<|13.44|> <|13.44|> and though they thought a ghost was approaching,<|17.08|> <|17.08|> and though they were afraid, they stayed together.<|22.08|> <|22.36|> They held onto each other,<|24.26|> <|24.26|> and they did not abandon their community.<|28.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It feels a little bit to me, it always has, that Peter does.<|7.60|> <|7.60|> And I appreciate Peter.<|9.02|> <|9.02|> He makes me feel better about being a lame Christian and the fact that I mess things up<|15.76|> <|15.76|> and if God can use Peter, then surely God can use me.<|20.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But Peter in this story, especially as the focus, which he often is, I don't like it.<|8.54|> <|8.54|> And that has not changed in six years.<|13.02|> <|13.02|> So for those of you who were here six years ago, I do not expect you to remember my sermon.<|19.68|> <|19.68|> As Richard has often said, no one ever walks out of church humming the sermon.<|26.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I do imagine those of you who were here remember the storms you weathered together leading up to that summer of 2017.<|13.36|> <|15.22|> And this is directly from my sermon in 2017, these two sentences.<|21.26|> <|21.26|> Amen.<|22.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The people of St. John's stayed in community.<|6.00|> <|6.00|> They stayed in the boat.<|9.24|> <|9.24|> Through several years of ups, downs, changes, interims,<|16.52|> <|16.52|> dramatic clergy, let's be honest, welcomes,<|21.52|> <|21.52|> farewells, and not an insignificant level of uncertainty.<|28.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Walking on water is not the only miracle in this story.<|8.16|> <|8.16|> So is the devotion of the disciples to one another<|12.40|> <|12.40|> and their trust that together they can weather a storm,<|18.80|> <|18.80|> just as many of you did oh so faithfully.<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If, as we believe, Scripture is alive and evolves and we evolve, and that somehow, miraculously,<|13.20|> <|13.86|> Scripture is always relevant to our present circumstance, what does this story compel me<|22.24|> <|22.24|> to notice now, today, after six years of being in the boat with you all?<|29.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There's a reason why where we are is called the nave.<|8.12|> <|8.12|> If you look up, you can see through the darkness and the lights blinding you, you can see the<|14.96|> <|14.96|> ribs of the ceiling.<|17.84|> <|17.84|> This is called a nave from the Latin word for ship.<|22.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And the ship or boat is an ancient symbol of the church.<|6.74|> <|7.82|> We are literally and figuratively in this boat together.<|14.02|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Father.<|1.68|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And as I said, I'm still not impressed with Peter, so that hasn't changed in six years.<|4.98|> <|6.26|> But I am still inspired by the model of community demonstrated by the disciples who remained<|13.62|> <|13.62|> together in the boat.<|14.68|> <|15.48|> And I still see that mightily evident here at St. John's as we love and support each<|22.82|> <|22.82|> other.<|23.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and work together to respond to so many of the cultural and personal tempests<|8.24|> <|8.24|> that have raged around us over the past six years.<|12.26|> <|13.40|> So we have stayed together through these cultural and personal tempests<|18.06|> <|18.06|> over the past six years.<|19.60|> <|20.10|> And I know many of you in this community feel that with each other.<|24.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And my friends, it can feel like there's always a storm raging, can't it?<|5.86|> <|7.84|> So while I'm not stepping out onto the waves,<|11.68|> <|12.48|> I am very happy to be bouncing around on the rough sea with you.<|16.12|> <|17.56|> And so what else am I noticing now in 2023 in this oh-so-familiar gospel text?<|24.82|> <|27.54|> Jesus.<|28.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus is who is grabbing my attention today.<|5.00|> <|7.20|> There is so much for us to learn about Jesus<|9.38|> <|9.38|> in this text from Matthew,<|11.12|> <|11.12|> so much that I needed to be reminded anew,<|14.58|> <|14.58|> made aware of for the first time,<|17.30|> <|17.30|> and promises about who Jesus is<|20.42|> <|21.42|> and what Jesus does.<|23.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For Matthew's audience, there would have been some very familiar allusions in this tale.<|8.56|> <|9.42|> This story is about Jesus walking on the water, not Peter, and what it means that Jesus walked on water.<|20.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus walking becomes a bridge between what God has done in the past and what God will do in the future through Christ.<|11.26|> <|11.26|> For those early Christians to whom Matthew wrote,<|16.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They would have recognized reverberations of Job in this story.<|6.36|> <|7.36|> Job declares that the creator traversed the oceans before the dawn of humanity, saying<|14.00|> <|14.00|> that God stretched out the heavens and trampled on the waves of the sea.<|18.92|> <|20.14|> Sound familiar?<|20.80|> <|20.80|> Yes.<|21.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Later, God questions Job about the cosmos and asks if he, Job, ever went upon the springs<|10.04|> <|10.04|> of the sea or walked in the recesses of the deep.<|15.22|> <|15.22|> Of course Job has not.<|18.72|> <|18.72|> Job is not God.<|20.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The disciples have already seen Jesus quiet a storm in chapter 8 of Matthew.<|8.60|> <|9.58|> And they wondered afterward, who could he possibly be that the wind and the sea obey him?<|17.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> At this moment, we find them in the boat<|3.70|> <|3.70|> immediately following the miraculous feeding of the 5,000.<|7.96|> <|8.32|> And they still aren't convinced.<|10.66|> <|11.44|> They're a little slow.<|12.42|> <|14.08|> But somehow...<|16.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Somehow, witnessing Jesus walking on water is what finally opens their eyes to Jesus' true identity,<|10.96|> <|12.18|> opens their hearts to find courage, and opens their mouths to worship him and proclaim,<|20.06|> <|20.06|> truly you are the Son of God.<|23.78|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> With this new understanding, one might say finally, of who Jesus is, they can cross the<|9.02|> <|9.02|> bridge to what God will do in the future through Christ.<|13.86|> <|14.74|> The disciples' pronouncement foreshadows the words of the centurion at the end in Matthew<|21.16|> <|21.16|> 27, at the foot of the cross, when that Roman centurion says, truly this was Jesus.<|29.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> the Son of God.<|2.00|> <|3.50|> The death upon the cross that was the redemption of all<|8.50|> <|8.50|> echoes and fulfills Peter's plea<|12.34|> <|12.34|> as he is sinking beneath the waves.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> Lord, save me.<|19.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.12|> As I think about what I learn about Jesus and from Jesus now,<|9.12|> <|9.40|> in the same gospel lesson that comes around every three years,<|13.06|> <|14.90|> I'm struck by how patient Jesus is with the disciples and with us.<|23.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Even when we are shown again and again who Jesus is, even when we fail again to recognize<|9.96|> <|9.96|> him, he appears.<|13.88|> <|13.88|> Against all human logic and understanding, Jesus delights in meeting us where we are.<|22.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> reminds us not to be afraid,<|3.60|> <|3.60|> reaches out to catch us,<|6.80|> <|6.80|> as the gospel says immediately,<|9.90|> <|9.90|> when we stumble or sink,<|13.90|> <|13.90|> crawls into the boat with us,<|18.90|> <|18.90|> and calms the chaos around us and within us.<|24.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus assures us that doubt is not the enemy<|5.00|> <|5.00|> or the opposite of faith.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> And even when we feel like we only have a little faith,<|13.00|> <|14.00|> the Gospel of Matthew has told us again and again,<|19.00|> <|19.00|> that can be enough, enough to produce great things,<|24.00|> <|25.00|> just like that mustard seed.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus does not abandon the disciples,<|5.00|> <|5.00|> and he will not abandon us.<|8.00|> <|10.00|> So my takeaway this time, six years later,<|14.00|> <|14.00|> yes, I'm still impressed with the disciples in the boat.<|18.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> but I am just so struck by the grace that is evident in how patient Jesus is.<|10.04|> <|10.78|> With our doubts, with our demands, with our dullness of understanding.<|18.18|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Isn't that good news?<|2.16|> <|3.84|> My friends, it is a good and a joyful thing<|9.18|> <|9.18|> to be in this boat together with you.<|12.82|> <|12.82|> Amen.<|14.46|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.50|> <|0.50|> Amen.<|1.04|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|2.94|><|endoftext|>
